text:
REFINED BRIEFING (events grouped by probative direction).
Retrieval hints: actors=['Secretary of State', 'Secretary of Defense', 'Chief of Staff', 'National Security Advisor', 'Press Secretary', 'Attorney General', 'Secretary of the Treasury', 'Secretary of Homeland Security', 'Deputy Chiefs of Staff', 'Assistants to the President', 'Counsel to the President', 'Director of the Office of Management and Budget', 'Director of National Intelligence', 'Director of the Central Intelligence Agency', 'Ambassador to the United Nations'], topics=['personnel changes', 'national security leaks', 'white house', 'cabinet', 'signal leak controversy'].

## BACKGROUND (12)
- [2025-01-20] Donald Trump began his second term as President of the United States on January 20, 2025, signing 26 executive orders. On that day, he declared a "national energy emergency" and initiated the United States' withdrawal from the 2015 Paris Climate Agreement. He signed Executive Order 14154, "Unleashing American Energy," directing federal agencies to revise regulations on domestic energy resources and revoking the American Climate Corps.

Other initial actions included initiating withdrawal from the World Health Organization, revising federal recognition of gender identity, establishing the Department of Government Efficiency, pardoning over 1,500 individuals involved in the January 6 Capitol riot, designating Mexican drug cartels as foreign terrorist organizations, and declaring a national emergency at the southern border.

The administration also announced cabinet and cabinet-level nominations, including Marco Rubio for Secretary of State and Pamela Bondi for Attorney General. Individuals with backgrounds as oil, gas, and chemical lobbyists were appointed to the Environmental Protection Agency. Some of these orders have been blocked in court.
- [2025-01-20] On January 20, 2025, President Trump announced 47 nominations to the Senate for various sub-cabinet and agency positions. These included Director of the National Institutes of Health, Deputy Attorney General, Commissioner of Social Security, Administrator of NASA, Director of the Federal Bureau of Investigation, and Surgeon General. Nominations also covered Secretaries for the Army, Air Force, and Navy, and Deputy Secretaries for Defense, State, Treasury, Homeland Security, and Agriculture. Many nominees were slated to replace resigned officials, with the Director of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention being a new role. All nominations are subject to Senate confirmation.

Concurrently, the administration announced key cabinet appointments. Marco Rubio was confirmed as Secretary of State, Pete Hegseth as Defense Secretary, and Robert F Kennedy Jr as Health Secretary. Pam Bondi was named Attorney General. Elon Musk was appointed to lead a new Department of Government Efficiency (Doge) in an advisory capacity, tasked with downsizing the federal government. Other cabinet positions filled included Interior, Homeland Security, Transportation, Energy, Commerce, Education, Treasury, Labor, Housing, and Agriculture. Dr Marty Makary was nominated to lead the Food and Drug Administration.
- [2025-01-20] Donald Trump was inaugurated as the 47th President of the United States on January 20, 2025, in the Capitol Rotunda, marking the start of his second non-consecutive term. JD Vance was simultaneously inaugurated as the 50th Vice President. On his first day in office, President Trump signed 26 executive orders and issued 12 memorandums. These initial actions included prohibiting the weaponization of federal agencies, repealing previous executive actions, banning federal censorship, ending the electric vehicle mandate, and initiating the second withdrawal process from the World Health Organization and the Paris Climate Accords.

Also on January 20, Susie Wiles assumed office as the 32nd White House Chief of Staff, becoming the first woman to hold the position. She succeeded Jeff Zients. President Trump also designated individuals to serve in acting capacities for various cabinet and cabinet-level positions, including Gary Washington as acting Secretary of Agriculture and Lisa Kenna as acting Secretary of State, pursuant to U.S. laws. The Republican Party retained its legislative majority in the House and secured the Senate majority in the 2024 elections.
- [2025-02-12] Tulsi Gabbard was confirmed by the U.S. Senate as the Director of National Intelligence (DNI) on February 12, 2025, by a 52-48 vote. President-elect Donald Trump nominated Gabbard on November 13, 2024. She replaced acting director Lora Shiao.

Gabbard, a former Democratic congresswoman who later became an independent and then a Republican, is the first female military combat veteran, first Pacific Islander American, and first Hindu American to hold the Cabinet-level DNI position. The DNI serves as the executive head of the United States Intelligence Community, overseeing 18 agencies including the CIA, FBI, and NSA, and manages a budget exceeding $70 billion. Her nomination drew scrutiny regarding her intelligence experience and past statements on government surveillance and foreign leaders.
- [2025-01-02] West Virginia Governor-elect Patrick Morrisey announced his initial cabinet appointments and executive team members during a press conference in Charleston on January 2, 2025. Morrisey named ten individuals to his executive team, including Tom McCaffery as Chief of Staff, Douglas P. Buffington II as Senior Advisor, and Crescent Gallagher as Press Secretary. Other executive appointments included A. Todd Johnston as Deputy Chief of Staff and Sean M. Whelan as General Counsel. Five individuals were appointed to cabinet roles, such as Senator Eric Nelson as Secretary of Revenue, Eric Householder as Secretary of Administration, and Alex Mayer as Secretary of Human Services. Harold Ward was retained as Secretary of the Department of Environmental Protection, and Dianna Graves was named Commissioner of the Bureau of Senior Services. Cabinet appointments are subject to approval by the legislative body.
- [2025-01-16] Senate confirmation hearings for President-elect Donald Trump's Cabinet nominees continued for a third day on Thursday, January 16, 2025.

Hearings began at 10 a.m. Eastern for Doug Burgum, nominated for Interior Secretary; Scott Turner, nominated for Housing and Urban Development Secretary; and Lee Zeldin, nominated for Environmental Protection Agency Administrator. Scott Bessent, nominated for Treasury Secretary, was scheduled for 10:30 a.m. Pam Bondi continued her hearing for Attorney General, with senators hearing from witnesses.

Bessent is a South Carolina billionaire. Burgum, a former North Dakota governor, is slated to chair a new National Energy Council. Turner served in the first Trump administration.

Earlier in the week, Pete Hegseth had a hearing for Defense Secretary, and Marco Rubio for Secretary of State on Wednesday. Kristi Noem, nominated for Homeland Security Secretary, is scheduled for a hearing on Friday.
- [2025-01-15] Six of President-elect Donald Trump's cabinet nominees are undergoing confirmation hearings on Capitol Hill. Senate Majority Whip John Barrasso expressed expectations for swift approval and bipartisan cooperation. Pete Hegseth, Trump's nominee for Secretary of Defense, was questioned in a hearing on January 14, 2025. Senator Joni Ernst publicly supported Hegseth. Florida Senator Marco Rubio was nominated for Secretary of State, and former Congressman Sean Duffy was nominated for Transportation Secretary. Pam Bondi, Trump's nominee for Attorney General, is expected to face questioning from Democrats regarding her past loyalty to Trump and her involvement in efforts to challenge the 2020 election results. Senate rules require a simple majority for confirmation, and Republicans hold 53 votes.
- [2025-01-14] President-elect Donald Trump announced several key appointments for his upcoming administration. Susie Wiles was named White House chief of staff, with Stephen Miller as her deputy. Elon Musk and Vivek Ramaswamy are slated to co-lead a new Department of Government Efficiency.

Other appointments include Marco Rubio as Secretary of State, Pam Bondi as Attorney-General, and Scott Bessent as Treasury Secretary. Pete Hegseth has been nominated for Defence Secretary, while Robert F Kennedy Jr. is set to become Health and Human Services Secretary. Tom Homan will take a role in border security, Tulsi Gabbard has been named Director of National Intelligence, and Kristi Noem for Secretary of Homeland Security. Mehmet Oz was named to lead the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services.

These appointments require Senate approval, where Republicans will hold a 53-47 majority. Confirmation hearings began on Tuesday.
- [2025-01-15] The Senate Judiciary Committee held the first day of confirmation hearings for Pam Bondi, President-elect Donald Trump's pick for Attorney General. Democrats questioned Bondi's independence from Trump and her stance on the legitimacy of the 2020 election. Lawmakers also raised questions regarding Trump's FBI pick, Kash Patel, citing his past promotion of unsubstantiated claims. Republicans used their time to criticize Justice Department investigations into Trump and their Democratic colleagues. Separate confirmation hearings were also held for Marco Rubio, nominated for Secretary of State; John Ratcliffe, nominated for CIA Director; Russell Vought, nominated for Office of Management and Budget Director; Sean Duffy, nominated for Transportation Secretary; and Chris Wright, nominated for Energy Secretary. Senator Patty Murray expressed strong opposition to Robert F. Kennedy Jr.'s nomination for Secretary of Health and Human Services.
- [2025-01-28] President-elect Donald Trump announced his intention to nominate Scott Bessent as the United States Secretary of the Treasury on November 22, 2024. Bessent appeared before the United States Senate Committee on Finance for his confirmation hearing on January 16, 2025, advocating for tax cut extensions and tougher economic policies on China and Russia. The U.S. Senate confirmed his nomination on January 27, 2025, by a 68–29 vote. Bessent was sworn in as the 79th U.S. Treasury Secretary on January 28, 2025. He is the first openly gay person to lead the U.S. Treasury Department and the first openly gay U.S. Senate-confirmed Cabinet member in a Republican administration.
- [2025-01-20] Donald Trump began his second term as President of the United States on January 20, 2025, signing 26 executive orders. On that day, he declared a "national energy emergency" and initiated the United States' withdrawal from the 2015 Paris Climate Agreement. He signed Executive Order 14154, "Unleashing American Energy," directing federal agencies to revise regulations on domestic energy resources and revoking the American Climate Corps.

The administration also announced cabinet and cabinet-level nominations, including Marco Rubio for Secretary of State and Pamela Bondi for Attorney General. Individuals with backgrounds as oil, gas, and chemical lobbyists were appointed to the Environmental Protection Agency. Some of these orders have been blocked in court.
